name: Mesh
classification_notes: Mesh is a geometric display typeface derived from 3D sculptural forms, referencing machine-readable typefaces (computrices) and science-fiction aesthetics; it sits firmly in the display category due to its extreme stylisation and perspective-driven bevel construction.
summary: A display typeface drawn from 3D sculptural letters originally built for a video game, referencing machine-legible computrice aesthetics and Vasarely's geometric world.
description: Designed by Marie-Mam Sai Bellier and published by Diorama Type Partners in 2023, Mesh originates from letter-sculptures created for the Diorama No.2 publication's accompanying video game, set in a geometric city inspired by Victor Vasarely. The 2D OTF version is derived by applying a consistent vanishing point to those 3D forms, producing a typeface dense with bevels that recall systematic modernism pushed to an absurd extreme. Each licence includes both the 2D and 3D versions, with the latter supplied as a Blender file.
foundry_blurb: Diorama Type Partners describe Mesh as originating from hybrid furniture sculptures and referencing Jean Alessandrini's Codex 80 concept of the computrice, an early machine-legible typeface absorbed into science-fiction aesthetics. The letters were first built as 3D game objects populating a Vasarely-inspired virtual world, then translated into a 2D typeface through a shared vanishing point. The typeface is sold as a single typographic project encompassing both 2D and 3D versions.

name: Astronef
designer: Jean Alessandrini, Marie-Mam Sai Bellier, Guillaume Sbalchiero
classification_notes: Astronef belongs to the 'machinales computrices' tradition of science-fiction-inspired geometric lettering from the 1970s, constructed in cut paper with hard angles and futuristic forms placing it firmly in display territory.
summary: A digitised revival of Jean Alessandrini's 1976 cut-paper typeface, rooted in the science-fiction-inflected 'machinales computrices' movement of the pre-1980s era.
description: Originally drawn in cut paper by Jean Alessandrini in 1976, the typeface sits within a specifically French tradition of futuristic lettering inspired by science fiction and post-war visions of new worlds. Developed digitally by Diorama Type Partners between 2020 and 2021 with Alessandrini's direct oversight, it was published in 2023 as an authorised updated version rather than a straight revival. The Latin Extended character set covers a wide range of languages across a single style, with stylistic set alternates for select letterforms.
foundry_blurb: Diorama Type Partners describe Astronef as a futuristic curiosity typical of the pre-1980s era, originally produced by Jean Alessandrini in 1976 as part of the 'machinales computrices typefaces' movement inspired by science fiction. It was digitally developed during the making of Revue Diorama No.2, Geometric City, with the extended Latin glyphset developed and validated by Alessandrini himself, and published not as a revival but as a genuine updated version of his original work.

name: Lezen
classification_notes: Lezen is a layered, multi-contour geometric typeface designed explicitly for titling and typographic experimentation, with no conventional text-weight application. Its modular, sculptural construction and layer-based system place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A 44-style layered geometric display family that pays tribute to Dutch designer Jurriaan Schrofer, built for typographic experimentation through superimposed contour layers.
description: Designed by Marie-Mam Sai Bellier and Guillaume Sbalchiero in tribute to the Dutch graphic designer Jurriaan Schrofer and his 1971 work 'Wie dit leest is gek', the family comprises four sub-families (Floor, City, Glow, Optical) each with eleven styles, totalling 44 styles that can be stacked and colour-mixed in layers. More than 400 glyphs per style and four stylistic sets shift the geometric complexity of the letterforms, allowing movement between legibility and sculptural abstraction. The system is designed with 3D translation in mind, reflecting Schrofer's own multidimensional approach to type as material.
foundry_blurb: Diorama Type Partners describe Lezen as a tribute to Jurriaan Schrofer and his 1971 work, presenting type as a malleable and sculptural material. The family's four layered sub-families (Floor, City, Glow, Optical) with 44 styles and over 400 glyphs per style are intended to open a territory of typographical experimentation across titling scales, with infinite compositional possibilities through colour-layered superposition and four stylistic sets that alter geometric complexity.

name: Mire
classification_notes: Mire is a display typeface derived from 19th-century wood-type letterforms, characterised by imposing serifs and extreme boldness typical of the wood-type printing era. It sits firmly in the display category, with ornamental glyphsets reinforcing its decorative, advertising-era heritage.
summary: An extra bold display typeface drawn from four 19th-century wood-type letters found in Michael Twyman's history of printing, extended with a large set of contemporary ornament glyphs.
description: Designed by Marie-Mam Sai Bellier between 2019 and 2022, Mire reconstructs a full alphabet from just four letters — M, I, R, E — sourced from a historical survey of print culture, with its defining character residing in the imposing serifs that frame each letterform. The name references both the source letters and the French idiom for having something in one's sights, a play on the display typeface's inherent desire to be seen. Four ornament sets accompany the single weight, allowing the typeface to be built into decorative frames and borders in the manner of Victorian job-printing.
foundry_blurb: Diorama Type Partners describe Mire as a display typeface developed from letters discovered in Michael Twyman's Printing: 1770–1970, evoking wood-type printing at the end of the 19th century and the flourishing of fancy typefaces at the birth of advertising. The team developed a large set of contemporary ornament glyphs alongside the typeface to allow playful construction of frames and decorative compositions.
